explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \sin x \text { is positive in the interval }(0, \pi) \text {. }\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \int_0^\pi\left|\sin ^3 x\right| \mathrm{d} x & =\int_0^\pi \sin ^3 x \mathrm{~d} x=\int_0^\pi \sin x\left(1-\cos ^2 x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^\pi \sin x \mathrm{~d} x+\int_0^\pi(-\sin x) \cos ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =[-\cos x]_0^\pi+\left[\frac{\cos ^3 x}{3}\right]_0^\pi=\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let\,\,\,\, I & =\int_0^1 \log (x+1) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =[\log (x+1) \cdot x]_0^1-\int_0^1 \frac{1}{x+1} \cdot x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =[x \log (x+1)]_0^1-\int_0^1\left(1-\frac{1}{1+x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =(\log 2-0)-[x-\log |1+x|]_0^1 \\ & =\log 2-(1-\log 2) \\ & =2 \log 2-1 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since }\left|x-\frac{1}{2}\right|=-\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right), \text { if } x<\frac{1}{2}\\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=x-\frac{1}{2}, \quad \text { if } x \geq \frac{1}{2}\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore & \int_0^1 x\left|x-\frac{1}{2}\right| \mathrm{d} x \\ & =-\int_0^{\frac{1}{2}} x\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x+\int_{\frac{1}{2}}^1 x\left(x-\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(\frac{1}{2} x-x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x+\int_{\frac{1}{2}}^1\left(x^2-\frac{1}{2} x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^{1 / 2}+\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^2}{4}\right]_{1 / 2}^1 \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{24}\right)+\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{16}-\frac{1}{24}\right)=\frac{1}{8} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{300 \sin x+100 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x=\ldots $$
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Since } \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{a \sin x+b \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x=\frac{\pi}{4}(a+b) \\ & \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{300 \sin x+100 \cos x}{\sin x+\cos x} d x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{4}(300+100) \\ & =100 \pi \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let } f(x)=\sin \left(\frac{e^x-1}{e^x+1}\right)\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(-x) & =\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^{-x}-1}{\mathrm{e}^{-x}+1}\right) \\ & =\sin \left(\frac{1-\mathrm{e}^x}{1+\mathrm{e}^x}\right) \\ & =\sin \left[-\left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^x-1}{\mathrm{e}^x+1}\right)\right] \\ & =-\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{e}^x-1}{\mathrm{e}^x+1}\right) \quad \ldots[\because \sin (-x)=-\sin x] \\ & =-\mathrm{f}(x) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^4 x}{\tan ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x}{\tan ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let \,\,\, I & =\int_1^3 \frac{\log x^2}{\log \left(16 x^2-8 x^3+x^4\right)} d x \\ & =\int_1^3 \frac{2 \log x}{\log \left[x^2\left(16-8 x+x^2\right)\right]} d x \\ & =\int_1^3 \frac{2 \log x}{\log x^2+\log \left(16-8 x+x^2\right)} d x \\ & =\int_1^3 \frac{2 \log x}{\log x^2+\log (4-x)^2} d x \\ & =\int_1^3 \frac{2 \log x}{2 \log x+2 \log (4-x)} d x \\ & =\int_1^3 \frac{\log x}{\log x+\log (4-x)} d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(3-1)=1 \\ & \cdots\left[\because \int_a^b \frac{f(x)}{f(x)+f(a+b-x)} d x=\frac{1}{2}(b-a)\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^1 \frac{1}{2+\sqrt{x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } 2+\sqrt{x}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} x=2(\mathrm{t}-2) \mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_2^3 \frac{2(\mathrm{t}-2)}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =2 \int_2^3\left(1-\frac{2}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \mathrm{dt} \\ & =2[\mathrm{t}-2 \log \mathrm{t}]_2^3 \\ & =2[1-2(\log 3-\log 2)] \\ & =2-4 \log \frac{3}{2} \\ & =2+4 \log \frac{2}{3} \\ & =2 \log \mathrm{e}+2 \log \frac{4}{9} \\ & =2 \log \left(\frac{4 \mathrm{e}}{9}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2+\log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \mathrm{~d} x+0 \ldots\left[\begin{array}{c} \because \log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \text { is an odd function } \\ \quad \text { and } x^2 \text { is an even function } \end{array}\right] \\ & =2\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{\pi^3}{12} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &Let\,\,\, \mathrm{I}=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1}{x}-1\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ &=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ &=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1-(1-x)}{1-x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \ldots\left[\because \int_0^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{f}(x)=\int_0^{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{a}-x) \mathrm{d} x\right] \\ &=\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right) \mathrm{dx} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Adding (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{I} & =\int_0^1\left[\log \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)+\log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right)\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1 \log \left(\frac{1-x}{x} \times \frac{x}{1-x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1 \log 1=0 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =0 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1}\left(1-x+x^2\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1-x(1-x)}{x+(1-x)}\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\cot ^{-1}\left[\frac{1-x(1-x)}{x+(1-x)}\right]\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+(1-x)}{1-x(1-x)}\right)\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[x]_0^1-\int_0^1\left[\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1}(1-x)\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x-\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1}(1-x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x-\int_0^1 \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x \left[\operatorname{In} 2^{\text {nd }} \text { integral } \int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\right] \end{aligned}\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \int_0^1 \tan ^{-1} x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-2\left\{\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_0^1-\int_0^1 \frac{1}{1+x^2} x \mathrm{~d} x\right\} \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-2\left\{\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_0^1-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left|1+x^2\right|\right]_0^1\right\} \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-2\left\{\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-0\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 2\right\} \\ & =\log 2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\sqrt{\tan x}+\sqrt{\cot x}) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\frac{\sqrt{\sin x}}{\sqrt{\cos x}}+\frac{\sqrt{\cos x}}{\sqrt{\sin x}}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{\sin x \cos x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\sqrt{2} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{2 \sin x \cos x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\sqrt{2} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\sqrt{1-(\sin x-\cos x)^2}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Put } & \sin x-\cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ \Rightarrow & (\sin x+\cos x) \mathrm{d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\\therefore \mathrm{I} & =\sqrt{2} \int_{-1}^0 \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{t}^2}} \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left[\sin ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{-1}^0 \\ & =\sqrt{2}\left[\sin ^{-1}(0)-\sin ^{-1}(-1)\right] \\ & =\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The value of the integral $\int_1^2 \frac{x \mathrm{~d} x}{(x+2)(x+3)}$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_1^2 \frac{x \mathrm{~d} x}{(x+2)(x+3)} \\ & \text { Let } \frac{x}{(x+2)(x+3)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x+2}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{x+3} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{A}(x+3)+\mathrm{B}(x+2) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=-2, \mathrm{~B}=3 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_1^2 \frac{-2}{x+2} \mathrm{~d} x+\int_1^2 \frac{3}{x+3} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =-2[\log (x+2)]_1^2+3[\log (x+3)]_1^2 \\ & =-2(\log 4-\log 3)+3(\log 5-\log 4) \\ & =-2 \log \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)+3 \log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right) \\ & =\log \left(\frac{5}{4}\right)^3-\log \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 \\ & =\log \frac{125}{64}-\log \frac{16}{9} \\ & =\log \frac{125}{64}+\log \frac{9}{16} \\ & =\log \frac{125 \times 9}{64 \times 16} \\ & =\log \frac{1125}{1024} \end{aligned} $$

Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{x}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$

By using property $\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x$

We get,

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=\int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{\pi-x}{1+\sin (\pi-x)} d x \\ & I=\int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{\pi-x}{1+\sin x} d x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Adding (i) and (ii), we got }\\ &\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{I} & =\int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{x}{1+\sin x}+\frac{\pi-x}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{\pi}{1+\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\pi \int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \frac{1}{1+\sin x} \times \frac{1-\sin x}{1-\sin x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\pi \int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi 3} \frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\pi \int_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3}\left(\sec ^2 x-\sec x \tan x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\pi[\tan x-\sec x]_{\pi / 3}^{2 \pi / 3} \\ & =\pi\left[\left(\tan \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)-\sec \left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)\right)-\left(\tan \frac{\pi}{3}-\sec \frac{\pi}{3}\right)\right] \\ & =\pi[(-\sqrt{3}+2)-(\sqrt{3}-2)] \\ & =\pi(-2 \sqrt{3}+4) \\ \quad 2 \mathrm{I} & =2 \pi(2-\sqrt{3}) \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\pi(2-\sqrt{3}) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Let } \mathrm{I} & =\int_0^2 \frac{3 x+1}{x^2+4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_0^2 \frac{3 x}{x^2+4} \mathrm{~d} x+\int_0^2 \frac{1}{x^2+4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2 & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Consider,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1=\int_0^2 \frac{3 x}{x^2+4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } x^2+4=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

When, $x=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=4$

$$ x=2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=8 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_1 & =\frac{3}{2} \int_4^8 \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}} \\ & =\frac{3}{2}[\log \mathrm{t}]_4^8 \\ & =\frac{3}{2}[\log 8-\log 4] \\ & =\frac{3}{2}[3 \log 2-2 \log 2] \\ & =\frac{3}{2}[\log 2] \\ & =\log 2^{3 / 2} \\ \mathrm{I}_1 & =\log 2 \sqrt{2} \end{aligned} $$

Now consider,

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{I}_2 & =\int_0^2 \frac{1}{x^2+4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_0^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-0\right] \\ \mathrm{I}_2 & =\frac{\pi}{8} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\log 2 \sqrt{2}+\frac{\pi}{8}\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \ldots[ From (i)]\end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned}Let\,\, I & =\int_{-1}^3\left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{x^2+1}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^2+1}{x}\right)\right) d x \\ & =\int_{-1}^3 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{x^2+1}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{x^2+1}\right) d x \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \mathrm{I} & =\int_{-1}^3 \frac{\pi}{2} \mathrm{~d} x \quad \ldots\left[\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}\right] \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2} \int_{-1}^3 1 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[x]_{-1}^3 \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[3-(-1)] \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}[4] \\ & =2 \pi \end{aligned} $$

content: $$ \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\cos ^2 x \sin ^2 x}{\cos ^3 x+\sin ^3 x} d x= $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let, }\\ &I=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\cos ^2 x \sin ^2 x}{\left(\cos ^3 x+\sin ^3 x\right)^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

Divide Nr and Dr by $\cos ^6 x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\frac{\sin ^2 x}{\cos ^4 x}}{\left(1+\frac{\sin ^3 x}{\cos ^3 x}\right)^2} d x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan ^2 x \cdot \sec ^2 x}{\left(1+\tan ^3 x\right)^2} d x \end{aligned} $$

Put, $1+\tan ^3 x=\mathrm{t}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 3 \tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^2 x \cdot \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{3} \end{aligned} $$

When, $x=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} x & =\frac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow t=2 \\ \therefore \quad I & =\frac{1}{3} \int_1^2 \frac{d t}{t^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\left[-\frac{1}{t}\right]_1^2=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{-1}{2}+1\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left[\frac{1}{2}\right] \\ I & =\frac{1}{6} \end{aligned} $$

content: $\int_2^4 \frac{\log x^2}{\log x^2+\log \left(36-12 x+x^2\right)} \mathrm{d} x$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_2^4 \frac{\log x^2}{\log x^2+\log \left(36-12 x+x^2\right)} \mathrm{d} x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I=\int_2^4 \frac{\log (6-x)^2}{\log (6-x)^2+\log x^2} \mathrm{~d} x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\\ \quad \text { }\left[\because \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=\int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-x) \mathrm{dx}\right] \end{aligned} $$

Adding (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_2^4 1 \mathrm{~d} x=[x]_2^4=4-2=2 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=1 \end{aligned} $$

Alternate Method:

Here, $\mathrm{f}(x)=\log x^2, \mathrm{a}=2$ and $\mathrm{b}=4$

$$ \begin{array}{r} \therefore \quad \int_2^4 \frac{\log x^2}{\log x^2+\log \left(36-12 x+x^2\right)} \mathrm{d} x=\frac{1}{2}(4-2)=1 \\ \ldots\left[\because \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \frac{\mathrm{f}(x)}{\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-x)} \mathrm{d} x=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~b}-\mathrm{a})\right] \end{array} $$

content: The value of $\int_0^2\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x$ is (where $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer function not greater than $x$ )
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \int_0^2\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x+\int_1^{\sqrt{2}}\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}}\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^2\left[x^2\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_0^1 0 \mathrm{~d} x+\int_1^{\sqrt{2}} 1 \mathrm{~d} x+\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{\sqrt{3}} 2 \mathrm{~d} x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^{\sqrt{2}} 3 \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =0+1(\sqrt{2}-1)+2(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})+3(2-\sqrt{3}) \\ & =\sqrt{2}-1+2 \sqrt{3}-2 \sqrt{2}+6-3 \sqrt{3} \\ & =5-\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^3 \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{(x+2) \sqrt{x+1}} \\ & \text { Put } x+1=\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} x=2 \mathrm{t} \mathrm{dt} \\ & \text { When } x=0, \mathrm{t}=1 \text { and when } x=3, \mathrm{t}=2 \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\int_1^2 \frac{2 t d t}{\left(t^2+1\right) \sqrt{t^2}} \\ & =2 \int_1^2 \frac{t d t}{\left(t^2+1\right) t} \\ & =2 \int_1^2 \frac{d t}{t^2+1} \\ & =2\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_1^2 \\ & =2\left(\tan ^{-1} 2-\tan ^{-1} 1\right) \\ & =2\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2-1}{1+(2)(1)}\right)\right] \left[\because \tan ^{-1} x-\tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-y}{1+x y}\right)\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{1+\cot ^{\mathrm{n}} x}=\frac{\pi}{4}\\ &\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{d x}{1+(\cot x)^{101}}=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \int_{-2}^2\left|x^2-x-2\right| \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_{-2}^{-1}\left(x^2-x-2\right) \mathrm{d} x+\int_{-1}^2-\left(x^2-x-2\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_{-2}^{-1}\left(x^2-x-2\right) \mathrm{d} x+\int_{-1}^2\left(-x^2+x+2\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}-\frac{x^2}{2}-2 x\right]_{-2}^{-1}+\left[\frac{-x^3}{3}+\frac{x^2}{2}+2 x\right]_{-1}^2 \\ & =\frac{-1}{3}-\frac{1}{2}+2-\left(\frac{-8}{3}-2+4\right)-\frac{8}{3}+\frac{4}{2}+4-\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{2}-2\right) \\ & =\frac{19}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { } \begin{aligned}Let\,\,\, \mathrm{f}(x) & =\sqrt{1+x+x^2}-\sqrt{1-x+x^2} \\ \mathrm{f}(-x) & =\sqrt{1-x+x^2}-\sqrt{1+x+x^2} \\ & =-\left(\sqrt{1+x+x^2}-\sqrt{1-x+x^2}\right) \\ & =-\mathrm{f}(x) \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) \text { is an odd function. }\\ &\therefore \quad \int_{-1}^1\left(\sqrt{1+x+x^2}-\sqrt{1-x+x^2}\right) d x=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The value of $\int_{\frac{1}{3}}^1 \frac{\left(x-x^3\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x^4} d x$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{1}{3}}^1 \frac{\left(x-x^3\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x^4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_{\frac{1}{3}}^1 \frac{x\left(\frac{1}{x^2}-1\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x^4} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_{\frac{1}{3}}^1 \frac{\left(\frac{1}{x^2}-1\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}{x^3} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \text { Put } \frac{1}{x^2}-1=\mathrm{t} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{-2}{x^3} \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{-1}{2} \int_8^0 \mathrm{t}^{\frac{1}{3}} \mathrm{dt} \\ =\frac{-1}{2}\left[\frac{3 \mathrm{t}^{\frac{4}{3}}}{4}\right]_8^0 \\ =\frac{-1}{2}\left(0-\frac{3}{4} 8^{\frac{4}{3}}\right) \\ =\frac{3}{8}\left(2^4\right) \\ =6 \mathrm{sq} \cdot \text { units } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: The value of $\int_{-3}^3 \sin ^7 x \cos ^{16} x \mathrm{~d} x$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^7 x \cdot \cos ^{16} x \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(-x)=[\sin (-x)]^7 \cdot[\cos (-x)]^{16} \\ & =[-\sin x]^7 \cdot[\cos x]^{16} \\ & =-\sin ^7 x \cdot \cos ^{16} x \\ & =-\mathrm{f}(x) \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) \text { is an odd function. }\\ &\therefore \quad \int_{-3}^3 \sin ^7 x \cos ^{16} x \mathrm{~d} x=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The value of $\int_1^4 \log [x] \mathrm{d} x$, where $[x]$ is the greatest integer function less than or equal to $x$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_1^4 \log [x] d x \\ & \begin{aligned} \int_1^4 \log [x] d x & =\int_1^2 0 d x+\int_2^3 \log 2 d x+\int_3^4 \log 3 d x \\ & =0+\log 2[3-2]+\log 3[4-3] \\ & =\log 2+\log 3 \\ & =\log (2 \times 3)=\log 6 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \int_1^e \frac{\mathrm{e}^x}{x}(1+x \log x) \mathrm{d} x & =\int_1^e \mathrm{e}^x\left(\frac{1}{x}+\log x\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\mathrm{e}^x \cdot \log x\right]_1^e \\ & =\mathrm{e}^e \end{aligned}$$

content: If $[x]$ denotes the greatest integer function, then $$\int_\limits0^5 x^2[x] d x=$$
explanation:
$\int_0^5 x^2[x] \mathrm{d} x=\int_0^1 x^2[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_1^2 x^2[x] \mathrm{d} x +\int_2^3 x^2[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_3^4 x^2[x]+\int_4^5 x^2[x] \mathrm{d} x$

$=\int_0^1 x^2(0) \mathrm{d} x+\int_1^2 x^2(1) \mathrm{d} x+\int_2^3 x^2(2) \mathrm{d} x +\int_3^4 x^2(3) d x+\int_4^5 x^2(4) d x$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_1^2+2\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_2^3+3\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_3^4+4\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_4^5 \\ & =\frac{1}{3}(8-1)+\frac{2}{3}(27-8)+(64-27)+\frac{4}{3}(125-64) \\ & =\frac{400}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $\int_\limits{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^2 x}{1+2^x} d x$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_\limits{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^2 x}{1+2^x} d x \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & I=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^2 x}{1+2^{-x}} d x\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$\ldots\left[\because \int_a^b \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=\int_a^b \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-x) \mathrm{d} x\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Adding (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}=2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$\ldots . \because \int_{-a}^a \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=2 \int_0^a \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x{ }^{\text {if }} \mathrm{f}(x)$ is an even function $]$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow I & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 x}{2}\right) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[x-\frac{\sin 2 x}{2}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \mathrm{I} & =\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{1+\cos x}}{(1-\cos x)^{\frac{5}{2}}} d x \\ & =\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sqrt{1+\cos x}}{(1-\cos x)^{\frac{5}{2}}} \times \frac{\sqrt{1-\cos x}}{\sqrt{1-\cos x}} d x \\ & =\int_{\pi / 3}^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x}{(1-\cos x)^3} d x \end{aligned}$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Put } 1-\cos x=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_{1 / 2}^1 \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}^3}=\left[\frac{\mathrm{t}^{-2}}{-2}\right]_{1 / 2}^1=\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of the integral $\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cot x}}{\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}} \mathrm{dx}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since } \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cot ^n x}{\tan ^n x+\cot ^n x} d x=\frac{\pi}{4} \text {, }\\ &\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sqrt{\cot x}}{\sqrt{\cot x}+\sqrt{\tan x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Put $1+\tan x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

When $x=0, \mathrm{t}=1$ and when $x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \mathrm{t}=2$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \int_0^{\pi / 4} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{(1+\tan x)(2+\tan x)} d x \\ & =\int_1^2 \frac{d t}{t(1+t)} \\ & =\int_1^2 \frac{d t}{t}-\int_1^2 \frac{d t}{1+t} \\ & =[\log t-\log (1+t)]_1^2 \\ & =\log _e 2-\log _e 3+\log _e 2 \\ & =\log _e\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of integral $\int_\limits{-2}^0\left(x^3+3 x^2+3 x+5+(x+1) \cos (x+1)\right) d x$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int_{-2}^0\left[x^3+3 x^2+3 x+5+(x+1) \cos (x+1)\right] d x \\ & =\int_{-2}^0\left[(x+1)^3+4+(x+1) \cos (x+1)\right] d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{rr} & \text { Put } x+1=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}=\int_{-1}^1\left(\mathrm{t}^3+4+\mathrm{t} \cos \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{dt} \end{array}\\ &\text { Since } t^3 \text { and } t \cos t \text { are odd functions. }\\ &\therefore \quad I=\int_{-1}^1 4 d t=4[t]_{-1}^1=8 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan x) \mathrm{d} x$, then value of $\mathrm{I}$ is
explanation:
Let $I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan \theta) d \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} &=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right] d \theta \\ & \ldots \cdot\left[\because \int_0^a f(x) d x=\int_0^a f(a-x) d x\right] \\ &=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left(1+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right) d \theta \\ &=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log 2 d \theta-\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan \theta) d \theta \\ & \therefore \quad 2 I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log 2 \mathrm{~d} \theta \Rightarrow I=\frac{\log 2}{2}[\theta]_0^{\pi / 4}=\frac{\pi}{8} \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int_\limits{0.2}^{3.5}[x] \mathrm{d} x=$$ (where $[x]=$ greatest integer not greater than $x$ )

explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left(\frac{\sin x+\cos x}{\cos x}\right) \mathrm{dx}\\ &\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan \theta) \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left[1+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right] d \theta \\ & \quad \ldots .\left[\because \int_0^a \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=\int_0^1 \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}-x) \mathrm{d} x\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log \left(1+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right) d \theta \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log 2 d \theta-\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log (1+\tan \theta) d \theta \\ \therefore \quad 2 I & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \log 2 d \theta \Rightarrow I=\frac{\log 2}{2}[\theta]_0^{\pi / 4}=\frac{\pi}{8} \log 2 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_0^a \frac{x-\mathrm{a}}{x+\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Let } x+\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mathrm{t}-\mathrm{a} \\ & \text { If } x=0 \text {, then } \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{a} \\ & \quad \text { If } x=\mathrm{a} \text {, then } \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{a} \\ & \therefore \quad \begin{aligned} \mathrm{d} x & =\mathrm{dt} \end{aligned} \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int_a^{2 \mathrm{a}} \frac{\mathrm{t}-2 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =\int_a^{2 \mathrm{a}} 1 \mathrm{dt}-2 \mathrm{a} \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{2 \mathrm{a}} \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}=[\mathrm{t}]_{\mathrm{a}}^{2 \mathrm{a}}-2 \mathrm{a}[\log \mathrm{t}]_{\mathrm{a}}^{2 \mathrm{a}} \\ & =\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{a}(\log 2 \mathrm{a}-\log \mathrm{a}) \\ & =\mathrm{a}-2 \mathrm{a} \log 2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\sin x)^{-4} d x$

$$=2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\sin x)^{-4} \mathrm{~d} x$$

$\ldots\left[\because(\sin x)^{-4}\right.$ is an even function $]$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^4 x}{\tan ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right) \sec ^2 x}{\tan ^4 x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Let $\tan x=\mathrm{t}$

when $x=0$, we get $\mathrm{t}=0$

when $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$, we get $\mathrm{t}=1$

Also, $\sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =2 \int_0^1 \frac{1+\mathrm{t}^2}{\mathrm{t}^4} \mathrm{dt} \\ & =2\left[\int_0^1 \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^4} \mathrm{dt}+\int_0^1 \frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^2} \mathrm{dt}\right] \\ & =2\left[\frac{\left[\mathrm{t}^{-3}\right]_0^1}{-3}+\frac{\left[\mathrm{t}^{-1}\right]_0^1}{-1}\right] \\ & =\frac{-8}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: $\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}|\sin x-\cos x| d x$ has the value
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}|\sin x-\cos x| d x \\ & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}-(\sin x-\cos x) d x+\int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x-\cos x) d x \end{aligned}$$

$$...\left\{ {\matrix{ {f(x) = f( - x)} & {0 \le x \le {\pi \over 4}} \cr { = f(x)} & {{\pi \over 4} \le x \le {\pi \over 2}} \cr } } \right\}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}(\cos x-\sin x) d x+\int_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin x-\cos x) d x \\ & =(\sin x+\cos x)_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}+(-\cos x-\sin x)_{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{4}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\sin 0-\cos 0\right] \\ & \quad-\left[\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-0-1\right]-\left[0+1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & =\left[\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}-1\right]-\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \\ & =\frac{4}{\sqrt{2}}-2 \\ & =\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{2}-2 \\ & =2 \sqrt{2}-2 \\ & =2(\sqrt{2}-1) \end{aligned}$$

content: The integral $\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{d x}{\sin 2 x\left(\tan ^5 x+\cot ^5 x\right)}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad I & =\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{d x}{\sin 2 x\left(\tan ^5 x+\cot ^5 x\right)} \\ & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{d x}{2 \sin x \cos x\left(\tan ^5 x+\frac{1}{\tan ^5 x}\right)} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan x\left(\frac{\tan ^{10} x+1}{\tan ^5 x}\right)} \mathrm{dx} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\tan ^4 x \sec ^2 x}{\tan ^{10} x+1} \mathrm{dx} \end{aligned}$$

Put $\tan ^5 x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow 5 \tan ^4 x \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{\frac{1}{9 \sqrt{3}}}^1 \frac{\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{5}}{\mathrm{t}^2+1} \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_{\frac{1}{9 \sqrt{3}}}^1 \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{9 \sqrt{3}}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{9 \sqrt{3}}\right)\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{x^2 \cos x}{1+\mathrm{e}^{-x}} \mathrm{~d} x$ is equal to
explanation:
$I=\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{x^2 \cos x}{1+\mathrm{e}^{-x}} \mathrm{dx}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$\mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\left(\frac{-\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)^2 \cdot \cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{1+\mathrm{e}^{\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)}}$

$\ldots\left[\int_\limits a^b f(x) d x=\int_\limits a^b f(a+b-x) d x\right]$

$=\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{x^2 \cos x}{1+e^x}\quad\text{..... (ii)}$

Adding equation (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\frac{x^2 \cos x}{1+\mathrm{e}^x}+\frac{x^2 \cos x}{1+\mathrm{e}^{-x}}\right) \mathrm{dx} \\ & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x\left[\frac{1}{1+\mathrm{e}^x}+\frac{1}{1+\mathrm{e}^{-x}}\right] \mathrm{dx} \\ & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x\left[\frac{1}{1+\mathrm{e}^x}+\frac{\mathrm{e}^x}{\mathrm{e}^x+1}\right] \mathrm{d} x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & 2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cdot \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ \therefore \quad & 2 \mathrm{I}=2 \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \quad \ldots\left[\begin{array}{l} \int_{-a}^a f(x)=2 \int_0^a f(x) d x \\ \text { if } f(x) \text { is even function } \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & =\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\left[x^2 \cdot \sin x-2 \int x \cdot \sin x \mathrm{~d} x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =\left[x^2 \sin x-2\left(-x \cos x+\int \cos x \mathrm{~d} x\right)\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =\left[x^2 \sin x+2 x \cos x-2 \sin x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =\left(\frac{\pi^2}{4}-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}-0+0-0\right) \\ & =\frac{\pi^2}{4}-2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin 2 x\left(\tan ^5 x+\cot ^5 x\right)} d x$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I} & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin 2 x\left(\tan ^5 x+\cot ^5 x\right)} \mathrm{dx} \\ & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\frac{2 \tan x}{1+\tan ^2 x}\left(\tan ^5 x+\frac{1}{\tan ^5 x}\right)} \mathrm{dx}\\ & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\left(1+\tan ^2 x\right)}{2 \tan x\left(\tan ^5 x+\frac{1}{\tan ^5 x}\right)} \mathrm{dx} \\ \mathrm{I} & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{2 \tan x\left(\tan ^5 x+\frac{1}{\tan ^5 x}\right)} \mathrm{dx} \end{aligned}$$

Let $\tan x=\mathrm{t}$

$$\sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

when $x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

when $x=\frac{\pi}{4}, \mathrm{t}=1$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & I=\int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1 \frac{d t}{2 t\left(t^5+\frac{1}{t^5}\right)} \\ & I=\int_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^1 \frac{t^4}{2\left(t^{10}+1\right)} d t \\ & \text { Let } t^5=u \\ \therefore \quad & 5 t^4 d t=d u \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \mathrm{u}=3^{\frac{-5}{2}} \\ & \text { When } \mathrm{t}=1, \mathrm{u}=1 \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{10} \int_\limits{\frac{-5}{2}}^1 \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\left(\mathrm{u}^2+1\right)} \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left(\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{u}\right)_{\frac{-5}{2}}^{3^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\tan ^{-1} 1-\tan ^{-1}\left(3^{\frac{-5}{2}}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{\left(3^{\frac{5}{2}}\right)}\right] \\ \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{10}\left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{9 \sqrt{3}}\right)\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The integral $\int_{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}}\left([x]+\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)\right) \mathrm{d} x$, where $[x]$ represent greatest integer function, equals
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}}\left([x]+\log _{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)\right) d x$

$$=\int_\limits{\frac{-1}{2}}^0[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}}[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \log _0\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right) \mathrm{d} x$$

Let $g(x)=\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)$

$$g(-x)=\log \left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right)=-\log \left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)=-g(x)$$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{g}(x)$ is a odd function.

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \int_\limits{\frac{-1}{2}}^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathrm{~g}(x) \mathrm{d} x=0 \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int_\limits{\frac{-1}{2}}^0(-1) \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}}(0) \mathrm{d} x+0 \\ & =[-x]_{\frac{-1}{2}}^0+0 \\ & =\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of the integral $\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(x^2+\log \frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x d x$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left[x^2+\log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right)\right] \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x \mathrm{~d} x+\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Let $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2$

Where $\mathrm{I}_1=\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x d x$ and

$$\mathrm{I}_2=\int_{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \mathrm{~d} x$$

Consider

$$\begin{aligned} I_1 & =\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x d x \\ & =2 \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} x^2 \cos x d x\quad \ldots\left[x^2 \cos x \text { is an even function }\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =2\left[x^2 \int \cos x \mathrm{~d} x-\int \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{~d} x}\left(x^2\right)\left(\int \cos x \mathrm{~d} x\right) \mathrm{d} x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =2\left[x^2 \cdot \sin x-\int 2 x \cdot \sin x \mathrm{~d} x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =2\left[x^2 \sin x-2 \int x \cdot \sin x \mathrm{~d} x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =2\left[x^2 \sin x-2\left(x(-\cos x)-\int(-\cos x) \mathrm{d} x\right)\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =2\left[x^2 \sin x+2 x \cos x-2 \sin x\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ & =2\left[\frac{\pi^2}{4} \sin \frac{\pi}{2}+2 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right. \left.-0^2 \sin 0-2 \times 0 \times \cos 0+2 \sin 0\right]\\ & =2\left[\frac{\pi^2}{4}-2-0-0-0\right] \\ & =2\left[\frac{\pi^2}{4}-2\right]=\frac{\pi^2}{2}-4 \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Consider }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_2=\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \mathrm{~d} x \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{I}_2=0 \quad \ldots\left[\log \left(\frac{\pi-x}{\pi+x}\right) \cos x \text { is an odd function }\right] \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi^2}{2}-4 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\tan \theta}{\sqrt{2 k \sec \theta}} d \theta=1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}},(k>0)$, then the value of $k$ is
explanation:
Let $I=\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\tan \theta}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{ksec} \theta}} d \theta$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{k}}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta} \times \sqrt{\cos \theta} \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{k}}} \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos \theta}} \mathrm{~d} \theta \end{aligned}$$

Let $\cos \theta=\mathrm{t}$

$$\therefore \quad \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta=-\mathrm{dt}$$

$\therefore \quad$ when $\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}, \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{2}$

when $\theta=0, t=1$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad I=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2 k}} \int_1^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}} \mathrm{dt} \\ &=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{2 k}} \frac{\left[t^{\frac{1}{2}}\right]_1^{\frac{1}{2}}}{\frac{1}{2}} \\ &=\frac{-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{k}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-1\right)=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{k}}\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & \text { Given that } I=\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{k}}=1 \Rightarrow k=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The value of $\mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\sqrt{\log _{\mathrm{e}}}}^{\sqrt{\log _{\mathrm{e}} 3}} \frac{x \sin x^2}{\sin x^2+\sin \left(\log _{\mathrm{e}} 6-x^2\right)} d x$ is
explanation:
Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_\limits{\sqrt{\log 2}}^{\sqrt{\log 3}} \frac{x \sin x^2}{\sin x^2+\sin \left(\log 6-x^2\right)} d x$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Put } x^2=t \Rightarrow 2 x d x=d t \\ \therefore \quad & I=\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits{\log 2}^{\log 3} \frac{\sin t}{\sin t+\sin (\log 6-t)} d t \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ \therefore \quad & I=\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits{\log 2}^{\log 3} \frac{\sin (\log 6-t)}{\sin (\log 6-t)+\sin t} d t\quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{array}$$

$\ldots\left[\because \int_\limits a^b f(x) d x=\int_\limits a^b f(a+b-x) d x\right]$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Adding (i) and (ii), we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits{\log 2}^{\log 3} \mathrm{dt}=\frac{1}{2}(\log 3-\log 2)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow I=\frac{1}{4} \log \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $f$ and $g$ be continuous functions on $[0, a]$ such that $f(x)=f(a-x)$ and $g(x)+g(a-x)=4$, then $\int_0^a f(x) g(x) d x$ is equal to
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=f(a-x) \\ & g(x)+g(a-x)=4 \end{aligned}$$

Let $\mathrm{I}=\int_0^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{g}(x) \mathrm{dx}$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^a f(a-x) \cdot g(a-x) d x \\ & =\int_0^a f(x) \cdot[(4-g(x)] d x \\ & =4 \int_0^a f(x) d x-\int_0^a f(x) g(x) d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=4 \int_0^a f(x) d x-I \\ & 2 I=4 \int_0^2 f(x) d x \\ \therefore \quad & I=2 \int_0^2 f(x) d x \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\tan ^{\mathrm{n}} x\right. & \left.+\tan ^{\mathrm{n}-2} x\right) \mathrm{d} x=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}-1} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_{12}+\mathrm{I}_{10} & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\left(\tan ^{12} \theta+\tan ^{10} \theta\right) \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & =\frac{1}{12-1} \\ & =\frac{1}{11} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x),-\frac{\pi}{2} < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$$ and $$f(0)=0$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(1)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime}(x) & =\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\left(\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}\right)^2}{\cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}-\sin ^2 \frac{x}{2}}\right] \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \frac{x}{2}+\sin \frac{x}{2}}{\cos \frac{x}{2}-\sin \frac{x}{2}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \frac{x}{2}}{1-\tan \frac{x}{2}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(x) & =\int\left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\frac{\pi x}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}+\mathrm{c} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}(0)=\mathrm{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=0 \quad ....[\because \text{f(0)=0 (given)]}\\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{\pi x}{4}+\frac{x^2}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(1)=\frac{\pi+1}{4} \end{array}$$

content: The integral $$\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \sec ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^{\frac{4}{3}} x d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\text { Let } I=\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \sec ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^{\frac{4}{3}} x \mathrm{~d} x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\cos ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \cdot \sin ^{\frac{4}{3}} x} \\ & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\frac{\sin ^{\frac{4}{3}} x}{\cos ^{\frac{4}{3}} x} \cdot \cos ^2 x} \\ & =\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{3}} \frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan^{\frac{4}{3}}x} d x \end{aligned}$$

Put $$\tan x=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\Rightarrow \sec ^2 x \mathrm{~d} x=\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\therefore \quad I=\int_\limits{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^{\sqrt{3}} \frac{d t}{t^{\frac{4}{3}}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left[-3 \mathrm{t}^{-\frac{1}{3}}\right]_{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}}^{\sqrt{3}} \\ & =-3\left[(\sqrt{3})^{\frac{-1}{3}}-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{\frac{-1}{3}}\right] \\ & =-3\left(3^{\frac{1}{6}}-3^{\frac{1}{6}}\right) \\ & =3^{\frac{7}{6}}-3^{\frac{5}{6}} \end{aligned}$$

content: The integral $$\int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \sec ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^{\frac{4}{3}} x d x$$ is equal to
explanation:
We have,

$$\begin{aligned} \int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \sec ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^{\frac{4}{3}} x d x &=\int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3}\left(\frac{1}{\cos x}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)^{\frac{4}{3}} d x \\ &=\int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{1}{\cos ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \sin ^{\frac{4}{3}} x} d x \\ &=\int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \frac{\sin ^{\frac{2}{3}} x}{\cos ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \sin ^2 x} d x \\ &=\int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \tan ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^2 x d x \end{aligned}$$

Put $\tan x=t$, then $\operatorname{cosec}^2 x d x=d t$. When $x=\frac{\pi}{6}, t=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ and when $x=\frac{\pi}{3}, t=\sqrt{3}$. Therefore,

$$\begin{aligned} \int_\limits{\pi / 6}^{\pi / 3} \tan ^{\frac{2}{3}} x \operatorname{cosec}^2 x d x &=\int_\limits{1 / \sqrt{3}}^{\sqrt{3}} t^{\frac{2}{3}} d t \\ &=\left[\frac{3}{5} t^{\frac{5}{3}}\right]_{1 / \sqrt{3}}^{\sqrt{3}} \\ &=\frac{3}{5}\left[(\sqrt{3})^{\frac{5}{3}}-\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{\frac{5}{3}}\right] \\ &=\frac{3}{5}\left[3^{\frac{5}{6}}-3^{-\frac{5}{6}}\right] \\ &=\frac{3}{5}\left[3^{\frac{5}{6}}-\frac{1}{3^{\frac{5}{6}}}\right] \\ &=\frac{3}{5}\left[\frac{3^{\frac{5}{6}} \cdot 3^{\frac{5}{6}}-1}{3^{\frac{5}{6}}}\right] \\ &=\frac{3}{5}\left[\frac{3^{\frac{5}{3}}-1}{3^{\frac{5}{6}}}\right] \\ &=\frac{3^{\frac{5}{3}}-1}{3^{\frac{1}{6}}} \\ &=\frac{3^{\frac{5}{3}}-1}{3^{\frac{1}{6}}} \cdot \frac{3^{\frac{1}{6}}}{3^{\frac{1}{6}}} \\ &=3^{\frac{7}{6}}-3^{\frac{1}{6}} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, the correct answer is Option B.

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{e}^{\cos x} \sin x & , \text { for }|x| \leq 2 \\ 2, & \text { otherwise }\end{array}\right.$$, then $$\int_\limits{-2}^3 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \int_\limits{-2}^3 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x & =\int_\limits{-2}^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits 2^3 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\int_\limits{-2}^2 \mathrm{e}^{\cos x} \sin x \mathrm{~d} x+\int_\limits2^3 2 \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Since $$\mathrm{e}^{\cos x} \sin x$$ is an odd function.

$$\therefore \quad \int_\limits{-2}^3 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=0+2(3-2)=2$$

content: The value of $$\int_\limits0^\pi\left|\sin x-\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right| \mathrm{d} x$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \int_\limits0^\pi\left|\sin x-\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right| d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}\left(\sin x-\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right) d x+\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi\left(\frac{2 x}{\pi}-\sin x\right) d x \\ & =[-\cos x]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}-\left[\frac{x^2}{\pi}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}+\left[\frac{x^2}{\pi}\right]_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi+[\cos x]_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi \\ & =(0+1)-\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-0\right)+\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+(-1-0) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Let $$f:[-1,2] \rightarrow[0, \infty)$$ be a continuous function such that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{f}(1-x), \forall x \in[-1,2]$$

Let $$\mathrm{R}_1=\int_{-1}^2 x \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x$$ and $$\mathrm{R}_2$$ be the area of the region bounded by $$y=\mathrm{f}(x), x=-1, x=2$$ and the $$\mathrm{X}$$-axis, then $$\mathrm{R}_2$$ is
explanation:
Given that $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{f}(1-x)$$ and $$\mathrm{R}_1=\int_{-1}^2 x \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad \mathrm{R}_1 =\int_\limits{-1}^2(1-x) \mathrm{f}(1-x) \mathrm{d} x \quad \ldots\left[\because \int_\limits{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=\int_\limits a^b \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-x) \mathrm{d} x\right] \\ \therefore \quad\mathrm{R}_1=\int_\limits{-1}^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x-\int_{-1}^2 x \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x \quad \ldots[\because \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{f}(1-x)] \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{R}_1=\int_\limits{-1}^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x-\mathrm{R}_1 \\ \therefore\quad 2 \mathrm{R}_1=\int_\limits{-1}^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x \end{array}$$

Note that $$\mathrm{R}_2=\int_\limits{-1}^2 \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{d} x=2 \mathrm{R}_1$$

Let $$I=\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{d x}{4+3 \cos x}$$

Put $$\tan \frac{x}{2}=\mathrm{t}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{d} x=\frac{2 \mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}$$ and $$\cos x=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{4+3 \cos x} & =\int_\limits0^{\infty} \frac{2 \mathrm{dt}}{7+\mathrm{t}^2} \\\\ & =\left[\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\sqrt{7}}\right)\right]_0^{\infty} \\\\ & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}}\left[\tan ^{-1} \infty-0\right] \\\\ & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{7}} \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{(1+x)^2} \mathrm{~d} x, x \geq 0$$, then $$\mathrm{f}(3)-\mathrm{f}(1)$$ is equal to

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ be positive for all real $$x$$. If $$\mathrm{I}_1=\int_\limits{1-\mathrm{h}}^{\mathrm{h}} x \mathrm{f}(x(1-x)) \mathrm{d} x$$ and $$\mathrm{I}_2=\int_\limits{1-\mathrm{h}}^{\mathrm{h}} \mathrm{f}(x(1-x)) \mathrm{d} x$$, where $$(2 h-1)>0$$, then $$\frac{I_1}{I_2}$$ is

content: Let $$\mathrm{f}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$$ and $$\mathrm{g}: \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$$ be continuous functions. Then the value of the integral $$\int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}(-x)][\mathrm{g}(x)-\mathrm{g}(-x)] \mathrm{d} x$$ is
explanation:
Let $$\mathrm{h}(x)=[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}(-x)][\mathrm{g}(x)-\mathrm{g}(-x)]$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{h}(-x) & =[\mathrm{f}(-x)+\mathrm{f}(x)][\mathrm{g}(-x)-\mathrm{g}(x)] \\ & =-[\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{f}(-x)][\mathrm{g}(x)-\mathrm{g}(-x)] \\ & =-\mathrm{h}(x) \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{h}(x)$$ is an odd function.

$$\therefore \quad \int_\limits{\frac{-\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \mathrm{~h}(x)=0$$

Adding (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{I}=\pi \int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\tan x}{\sec x+\cos x} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} \mathrm{~d} x \end{aligned}$$

Put $$\cos x=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sin x \mathrm{~d} x=-\mathrm{dt}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =-\frac{\pi}{2} \int_\limits1^{-1} \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & =-\frac{\pi}{2}\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_1^{-1} \\ & =\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi^2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int_\limits0^1\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)(1) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\cos ^{-1} x \cdot x-\int\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \cdot x\right) \mathrm{d} x\right]_0^1 \\ & =\left[x \cdot \cos ^{-1} x+\int \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \mathrm{~d} x\right]_0^1 \\ & =\left[x \cos ^{-1} x-\sqrt{1-x^2}\right]_0^1 \\ & =\left[1 \cdot \cos ^{-1}(1)-\sqrt{1-(1)^2}\right]-\left[0 \cdot \cos ^{-1}(0)-\sqrt{1-0^2}\right] \\ & =0-(-1) \\ & =1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{x^2}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{6}$$, then the value of $$\mathrm{k}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{x^2}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & \text { Put } x=\sin \theta \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow d x= & \cos \theta d \theta \\ \left(1-x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} & =\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} \\ & =\left(\cos ^2 \theta\right)^{\frac{3}{2}} \\ & =\cos ^3 \theta \\ \therefore \quad I & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{\sin ^2 \theta \cdot \cos \theta d \theta}{\cos ^3 \theta} \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \tan ^2 \theta d \theta \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}}\left(\sec ^2 \theta-1\right) d \theta \\ & =[\tan \theta]_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}}-[\theta]_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \\ & =\left(\tan \frac{\pi}{6}-\tan 0\right)-\left(\frac{\pi}{6}-0\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}-\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}-\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & =\frac{2 \sqrt{3}-\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

But, $$\int_\limits0^{\frac{1}{2}} \frac{x^2}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \mathrm{~d} x=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{6}$$ $$\quad$$ ...... [Given]

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{k}=2 \sqrt{3}-\pi$$

Given $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=x-\frac{5}{x^5}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Integrating both sides, we get

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{f}(x)=\int\left(x-\frac{5}{x^5}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{5}{4} \times \frac{1}{x^4}+\mathrm{c} \\ \therefore \quad & \text { But } \mathrm{f}(1)=4 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{1}{2}+\frac{5}{4}+\mathrm{c}=4 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{c}=\frac{9}{4} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{f}(x)=\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{5}{4} \frac{1}{x^4}+\frac{9}{4} \end{array}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \mathrm{I}=\int_\limits1^2 \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\left(x^2-2 x+4\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ & =\int_\limits1^2 \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\left[(x-1)^2+3\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ & \text { Put } x-1=\sqrt{3} \tan \theta \\ & \mathrm{d} x=\sqrt{3} \sec ^2 \theta \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & \text { When } x=1, \theta=0 \\ & \text { When } x=2, \theta=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{I} & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{\sqrt{3} \sec ^2 \theta}{\left[3 \tan ^2 \theta+3\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}} \mathrm{~d} \theta \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{\sqrt{3} \sec ^2 \theta}{\left[3\left(1+\tan ^2 \theta\right)\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{\sqrt{3} \sec ^2 \theta}{3 \cdot \sqrt{3}\left(\sec ^2 \theta\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{\sec { }^2 \theta}{\sec ^3 \theta} \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \cos \theta \\ & =\frac{1}{3}[\sin \theta]_0^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \\ \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{3}\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{6}-\sin 0\right] \\ \mathrm{I} & =\frac{1}{6} \\ \therefore &\quad \frac{k}{k+5}=\frac{1}{6} \\ \quad &6k=k+5\\ \therefore & \quad k=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\mathrm{f}(x)$$ is a function satisfying $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{f}(x)$$ with $$\mathrm{f}(0)=1$$ and $$\mathrm{g}(x)$$ is a function that satisfies $$\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{g}(x)=x^2$$. Then the value of the integral $$\int_\limits0^1 f(x) g(x) d x$$ is
explanation:
As $$\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)=\mathrm{f}(x)$$

$$\frac{\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(x)}{\mathrm{f}(x)}=1$$

Integrating on both sides, we get

$$\log \mathrm{f}(x)=x+\mathrm{c}$$ ..... (i)

As $$\mathrm{f}(0)=1$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \text { (i) } \Rightarrow \mathrm{c}=0 \\ \therefore & \log \mathrm{f}(x)=x \\ \therefore & \mathrm{f}(x)=\mathrm{e}^x \end{array}$$

As $$\mathrm{f}(x)+\mathrm{g}(x)=x^2$$

$$\mathrm{g}(x)=x^2-\mathrm{e}^x$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{f}(x) \mathrm{g}(x) & =\mathrm{e}^x\left(x^2-\mathrm{e}^x\right) \\ & =\int_\limits0^1\left(\mathrm{e}^x x^2-\mathrm{e}^{2 x}\right) \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\left(x^2-2 x+2\right) \mathrm{e}^x\right]_0^1-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{e}^2+\frac{1}{2} \\ & =\mathrm{e}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{e}^2-\frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$I=\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{3 \pi}{4}} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{1+\cos x}=\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{3 \pi}{4}} \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{2 \cdot \cos ^2 \frac{x}{2}}$$

$$=\int_\limits{\frac{\pi}{4}}^{\frac{3 \pi}{4}}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2}\right) \mathrm{d} x=\left[\tan \frac{x}{2}\right]_{\pi / 4}^{3 \pi / 4}$$

$$=\tan \frac{3 \pi}{8}-\tan \frac{\pi}{8}=\frac{\sin (3 \pi / 8)}{\sin (3 \pi / 8)}-\frac{\sin (\pi / 8)}{\cos (\pi / 8)}$$

$$=\frac{\sin (3 \pi / 8) \cdot \cos (\pi / 8)-\cos (3 \pi / 8) \cdot \sin (\pi / 8)}{\cos (3 \pi / 8) \cdot \cos (\pi / 8)}$$

$$=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{\pi}{8}\right)}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}+\frac{\pi}{8}\right)+\cos \left(\frac{3 \pi}{8}-\frac{\pi}{8}\right)\right]}$$

$$=\frac{2 \times \sin \frac{\pi}{4}}{\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\cos \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{0+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}=2$$

content: The value of the integral $$\int_\limits0^1 \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}} \mathrm{~d} x$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &I=\int_\limits0^1 \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x}} \mathrm{~d} x=\int_\limits0^1 \sqrt{\frac{1-x}{1+x} \cdot \frac{1-x}{1-x}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_\limits0^1 \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \mathrm{~d} x \\ & =\int_\limits0^1\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right] \mathrm{d} x \\ & =\left[\sin ^{-1} x+\sqrt{1-x^2}\right]_0^1 \\ & =\left(\sin ^{-1} 1+0\right)-\left(\sin ^{-1} 0+1\right) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-(0+1) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-1 \end{aligned}$$

content:
$$\int_\limits0^2[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits0^2|x-1| \mathrm{d} x=$$

(where $$[x]$$ denotes the greatest integer function.)
explanation:
To evaluate the given integrals, let's tackle them one by one.

First, let's evaluate the integral with the greatest integer function (also known as the floor function), $$\int_\limits0^2[x] \mathrm{d} x$$.

The greatest integer function $$[x]$$ returns the greatest integer less than or equal to $$x$$. On the interval from 0 to 2, $$[x]$$ takes on the values 0 and 1. Specifically:

  • For $$0 \le x < 1, [x] = 0$$
  • For $$1 \le x < 2, [x] = 1$$

Hence, the integral of the greatest integer function can be separated into two integrals:

$$\int_\limits0^2[x] \mathrm{d} x = \int_\limits0^1 0\,\mathrm{d} x + \int_\limits1^2 1\,\mathrm{d} x$$

Evaluating these integrals, we get:

$$= \big(0 \cdot x\big) \Big|_0^1 + \big(x\big) \Big|_1^2$$ $$= (0 \cdot 1 - 0 \cdot 0) + (2 - 1)$$ $$= 0 + 1$$ $$= 1$$

Next, let's evaluate the integral of the absolute value function, $$\int_\limits0^2|x-1| \mathrm{d} x$$.

The absolute function $$|x - 1|$$ equals $$x - 1$$ when $$x \ge 1$$ and equals $$-x + 1$$ when $$x < 1$$. Consequently, the integral can be separated at the point where $$x = 1$$:

$$\int_\limits0^2|x-1| \mathrm{d} x = \int_\limits0^1(-x+1) \mathrm{d} x + \int_\limits1^2(x-1) \mathrm{d} x$$

Evaluating the first part of the integral:

$$\int_\limits0^1(-x+1) \mathrm{d} x = \left(-\frac{x^2}{2} + x\right) \Big|_0^1$$ $$= \left(-\frac{1^2}{2} + 1\right) - \left(-\frac{0^2}{2} + 0\right)$$ $$= (-\frac{1}{2} + 1) - 0$$ $$= \frac{1}{2}$$

Now, evaluating the second part:

$$\int_\limits1^2(x-1) \mathrm{d} x = \left(\frac{x^2}{2} - x\right) \Big|_1^2$$ $$= \left(\frac{2^2}{2} - 2\right) - \left(\frac{1^2}{2} - 1\right)$$ $$= (2 - 2) - (\frac{1}{2} - 1)$$ $$= 0 - (-\frac{1}{2})$$ $$= \frac{1}{2}$$

Adding the results of both parts of the absolute value integral:

$$\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = 1$$

Finally, combining the results from both original integrals:

$$\int_\limits0^2[x] \mathrm{d} x+\int_\limits0^2|x-1| \mathrm{d} x = 1 + 1 = 2$$

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D, which is 2.

content: $$\int_\limits2^5 2[\mathrm{x}] \mathrm{dx}=\{\text { where }[\mathrm{x}] \text { denotes the greatest integer function } \leq \mathrm{x}\}$$
explanation:
Let

$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int_\limits2^5 2[x] d x \\ & =\int_\limits2^3 2(2) d x+\int_\limits3^4 2(3) d x+\int_\limits4^5 2(4) d x \\ & =4[x]_2^3+6[x]_3^4+8[x]_4^5=4+6+8=18 \end{aligned}$$

Let

$$\begin{aligned} I & =\int_\limits0^\pi x \sin x \cos ^4 x d x \quad \text{... (1)}\\ & =\int_\limits0^\pi(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)[\cos (\pi-x)]^4 d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^\pi(\pi-x) \sin x[\cos x]^4 d x \quad \text{... (2)} \end{aligned}$$

Eq. (1) + (2) gives,

$$2 I=\int_\limits0^\pi \pi \sin x \cos ^4 x d x$$

Put $$\cos x=t \Rightarrow-\sin x d x=d t$$

When $$x=0, t=1$$ and when $$x=\pi, t=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{I} & =\pi \int_\limits1^{-1}(\mathrm{t})^4(-\mathrm{dt}) \\ & =\pi \int_\limits{-1}^1 \mathrm{t}^4 \mathrm{dt} \quad=2 \pi \int_\limits0^1 \mathrm{t}^4 d t \quad \text{...[t}^4 \text{is an even function]}\\ \therefore 2 \mathrm{I} & =\frac{2 \pi}{5}\left[\mathrm{t}^5\right]_0^1 \Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content: $$\int_\limits0^4 x[x] d x=$$ (where $$[\mathrm{x}]$$ denotes greatest integer function not greater than $$\mathrm{x}]$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } I & =\int_\limits0^4 x[x] d x \\ \therefore I & =\int_\limits0^1(0) d x+\int_\limits1^2 x d x+\int_\limits2^3 2 x d x+\int_\limits3^4 3 x d x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{2 x^2}{2}\right]_2^3+\left[\frac{3 x^2}{2}\right]_3^4=\frac{1}{2}(4-1)+(9-4)+\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)(16-9)=\frac{3}{2}+5+\frac{21}{2} \\ & =17 \end{aligned}$$

$$\text { Let } \quad I=\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left(\frac{4+3 \sin x}{4+3 \cos x}\right) d x \quad \text{.... (1)}$$

$$=\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left[\frac{4+3 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{4+3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}\right] d x=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \left[\frac{4+3 \cos x}{4+3 \sin x}\right] d x\quad \text{.... (2)}$$

Eq. (1) + (2) gives,

$$2 I=\log \left[\frac{4+3 \sin x}{4+3 \cos x} \times \frac{4+3 \cos x}{4+3 \sin x}\right] d x \quad=\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\log 1) d x=0$$

Let $$\quad I=\int_\limits0^\pi \frac{1}{4+3 \cos x} d x$$

Put $$\tan \frac{x}{2}=t \Rightarrow \cos x=\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2} \quad$$ and $$\quad \sec ^2 \frac{x}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) d x=d t \Rightarrow d t=\frac{2}{1+t^2} d t$$

When $$\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{t}=0$$ and when $$\mathrm{x}=\pi, \mathrm{t}=\infty$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore I & =\int_\limits0^{\infty} \frac{1}{4+3\left(\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}\right)} \times \frac{2}{1+t^2} d t \\ & =\int_\limits0^{\infty} \frac{\left(1+t^2\right)}{4\left(1+t^2\right)+3\left(1-t^2\right)} \times \frac{2}{1+t^2} d t=\int_\limits0^{\infty} \frac{2}{7+t^2} d t=\frac{2}{7} \int_\limits0^{\infty} \frac{d t}{1+\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{7}}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{2}{7}\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{t}{7}\right)\right]_0^{\infty} \times \frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{7}}\right)}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}}\left[\tan ^{-1} \infty-\tan ^{-1} 0\right]=\frac{2}{\sqrt{7}} \times \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{\pi}{\sqrt{7}} \end{aligned}$$

Let

$$\begin{aligned} & I =\int_\limits1^3\left[\tan ^1\left(\frac{x}{x^2-1}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x^2-1}{x}\right)\right] d x \\ & =\int_\limits1^3\left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{x^2-1}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{x^2-1}\right)\right] d x \\ & =\int_\limits1^3\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) d x=\frac{\pi}{2} \int_\limits1^3 d x=\frac{\pi}{2}[x]_1^3=\pi \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_0^1|5 x-3| d x \\ & 5 x-3=0 \quad \Rightarrow x=\frac{3}{5} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore I & =\int_0^{\frac{3}{5}}-(5 x-3) d x+\int_{\frac{3}{5}}^1(5 x-3) d x \\ & =\frac{-5}{2}\left[x^2\right]_0^{\frac{3}{5}}+3[x]_0^{\frac{3}{5}}+\frac{5}{2}\left[x^2\right]_{\frac{3}{5}}^1-3[x]_{\frac{3}{5}}^1 \\ & =\left(\frac{-5}{2}\right)\left(\frac{9}{25}\right)+3\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)+\frac{5}{2}\left(1-\frac{9}{25}\right)-3\left(1-\frac{3}{5}\right) \\ & =\frac{-45}{50}+\frac{9}{5}+\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)\left(\frac{16}{25}\right)-3\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)=\frac{-45}{50}+\frac{3}{5}+\frac{8}{5}=\frac{-45+110}{50}=\frac{65}{50}=\frac{13}{10} \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\quad I=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos x}{3 \cos x+\sin x} d x$$

Put $$\cos x=A(3 \cos x+\sin x)+B \frac{d}{d x}(3 \cos x+\sin x)$$

$$=A(3 \cos x+\sin x)+B(-3 \sin x+\cos x)$$

$$=\cos x(3 A+B)+\sin x(A-3 B)$$

Thus $$3 \mathrm{~A}+\mathrm{B}=1$$ and $$\mathrm{A}-3 \mathrm{~B}=0$$

Solving, we get $$B=\frac{1}{10}, A=\frac{3}{10}$$

$$\therefore I=\int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\frac{3}{10}(3 \cos x+\sin x)+\frac{1}{10}(-3 \sin x+\cos x)}{3 \cos x+\sin x} d x$$

$$=\frac{3}{10} \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} d x+\frac{1}{10} \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\frac{d}{d x}(3 \cos x+\sin x)}{3 \cos x+\sin x} d x$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{3}{10}[x]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}+\frac{1}{10}[\log |3 \cos x+\sin x|]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{3}{10}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+\frac{1}{10}[\log |1|-\log |3|] \\ & =\frac{3 \pi}{20}-\frac{1}{10} \log 3 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\quad I=\int_{\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\operatorname{cosec} x \cot x}{1+\operatorname{cosec}^2 x} d x$$

Put $$\operatorname{cosec} x=t \Rightarrow \operatorname{cosec} x \cot x=-d t$$. When $$x=\frac{\pi}{6}, t=2$$ and when $$x=\frac{\pi}{2}, t=1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore I & =\int_\limits2^1(-1) \frac{d t}{1+t^2} \\ & =\int_\limits1^2 \frac{d t}{1+t^2}=\left[\tan ^{-1} t\right]_1^2=\tan ^{-1}(2)-\tan ^{-1}(1)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2-1}{1+(2)(1)}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\quad$$ $$I=\int_\limits0^2|2 x-3| d x$$

When $$\mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{2}, 2 \mathrm{x}-3=0$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore I & =\int_\limits0^{3 / 2}(3-2 x) d x+\int_\limits{\frac{3}{2}}^2(2 x-3) d x=[3 x]_0^{3 / 2}-\frac{2}{2}\left[x^2\right]_0^{3 / 2}+\frac{2}{2}\left[x^2\right]_{3 / 2}^2-[3 x]_{3 / 2}^2 \\ & =\left(\frac{9}{2}\right)-\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)+\left(4-\frac{9}{4}\right)-3\left(2-\frac{3}{2}\right)=\frac{5}{2} \end{aligned}$$

We have $$\int_\limits0^a \sqrt{\frac{a-x}{x}} d x=\frac{k}{2}$$

Put $$x=a \sin ^2 \theta \Rightarrow d x=a(2 \sin \theta) \cos \theta d \theta=2 a \sin \theta \cos \theta d \theta$$

When $$\mathrm{x}=0, \theta=0$$ and when $$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}, \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{a-a \sin ^2 \theta}{a \sin ^2 \theta}}(2 a \sin \theta \cos \theta) d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \\ & \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sqrt{\frac{\cos ^2 \theta}{\sin ^2 \theta}} 2(a \sin \theta \cos \theta) d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \Rightarrow \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}(2 a \sin \theta \cos \theta) d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \\ & \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 2 a \cos \theta^2 d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \Rightarrow 2 a \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1+\cos 2 \theta}{2} d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \\ & a \int_\limits0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(1+\cos 2 \theta) d \theta=\frac{k}{2} \Rightarrow a\left[\theta+\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{2}\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{k}{2} \\ & a\left[\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+0\right)-(0+0)\right]=\frac{k}{2} \Rightarrow a \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{k}{2} \Rightarrow k=\pi a \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } f(x)=\frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} \\ & f(-x)=\frac{(-x) \sin (-x)}{1+\cos ^2 x}=\frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} \\ & \therefore f(x)=f(-x) \Rightarrow f(x) \text { is an even function } \\ & \text { Let } \quad I=\int_\limits{-\pi}^\pi \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} d x\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore I & =2 \int_\limits0^\pi \frac{x \sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} d x \quad \text{.....(1)}\\ & =2 \int_\limits\limits0^\pi \frac{(\pi-x) \sin (\pi-x)}{1+[\cos (\pi-x)]^2} d x=2 \int_\limits\limits0^\pi \frac{(\pi-x) \sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} d x \quad \text{.....(2)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{Eq}(1)+(2)$$ gives,

$$2 I=2 \pi \int_\limits0^\pi \frac{\sin x}{1+\cos ^2 x} d x$$

Put $$\cos \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \sin \mathrm{x} d \mathrm{x}=-\mathrm{dt}$$. Also when $$\mathrm{x}=0, \mathrm{t}=1$$ and when $$\mathrm{x}=\pi, \mathrm{t}=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore 2 \mathrm{I} & =2 \pi \int_\limits1^{-1} \frac{-\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \\ & =2 \pi \int_\limits{-1}^1 \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}=4 \pi \int_\limits0^1 \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}=4 \pi\left[\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t}\right]_0^1=4 \pi\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\pi^2 \\ \therefore I & =\frac{\pi^2}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Let $$\begin{aligned} I & =\int_\limits0^1 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x-1}{1+x-x^2}\right) d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^1 \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{x+(x-1)}{1+x(1-x)}\right] d x=\int_\limits0^1 \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{x+(x-1)}{1-(x-1)(x)}\right] d x \\ & =\int_\limits0^1\left[\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1}(x-1)\right] d x=\int_\limits0^1 \tan ^{-1} x d x+\int_\limits0^1 \tan ^{-1}(x-1) d x \\ & =\left[\left[x \tan ^{-1} x\right]_0^1-\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits0^1 \frac{2 x}{1+x^2} d x\right]+\left[\left[x \tan ^{-1}(x-1)\right]_0^1-\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits0^1 \frac{2 x-2+2}{1+(x-1)^2} d x\right]\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left|1+\mathrm{x}^2\right|\right]_0^1+0-\frac{1}{2} \int_\limits0^1 \frac{2 \mathrm{x}-2}{1+(\mathrm{x}-1)^2} \mathrm{dx}-\int_\limits0^1 \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{1+(\mathrm{x}-1)^2} \\ & =\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \log 2-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left|1+(\mathrm{x}-1)^2\right|\right]_0^1-\left[\tan ^{-1}(\mathrm{x}-1)\right]_0^1 \\ & =\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log 2-\frac{1}{2}(0-\log 2)-\left[0-\tan ^{-1}(-1)\right] \\ & =\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \log 2+\frac{1}{2} \log 2-\frac{\pi}{4}=0\end{aligned}$$

Let
